nodes.title: Guard against missing roots
nodes.why: If one tree is missing, there is no valid twin to return.
nodes.alternative: Throw an error when either root is missing.
nodes.tradeoff: Returning null keeps the API simple, but hides whether input was invalid or just not found.

nodes.title: Return immediately when target is current node
nodes.why: Both trees share structure, so matching position in A maps directly to the same position in B.
nodes.alternative: Continue traversing children even after a match.
nodes.tradeoff: Early return is faster and clearer, but only works because structural parity is assumed.

nodes.title: Traverse both trees in lockstep
nodes.why: Keeping paired child arrays ensures each recursive call compares equivalent positions.
nodes.alternative: Search tree B independently using selectors or node values.
nodes.tradeoff: Lockstep traversal is reliable for twin trees, but depends on the trees staying structurally identical.

nodes.title: Recurse by matching child index
nodes.why: The index path from root to target in tree A is exactly the path to the twin in tree B.
nodes.alternative: First compute the full path in A, then replay path in B.
nodes.tradeoff: Single-pass recursion is concise, while path-based two-pass logic can be easier to debug.

nodes.title: Stop searching after first match
nodes.why: Once we find the twin, there is no reason to scan the rest of the tree.
nodes.tradeoff: Short-circuit improves performance, but does not gather extra diagnostics about remaining branches.

nodes.title: Return null when target is not in tree A
nodes.why: A null fallback gives callers a clear signal that no corresponding node exists.
nodes.alternative: Throw when target is not found.
nodes.tradeoff: Null is easy to handle in flow control, but callers must remember null checks.
